era_context: The High Middle Ages in Europe; the papacy under Gregory IX asserts authority against secular rulers. The Almohad dynasty is in decline; the Reconquista accelerates in Iberia. In East Asia, the Jin dynasty faces intensifying Mongol pressure under Genghis Khan; the Song dynasty controls southern China. The Khmer Empire remains dominant in Southeast Asia. Islamic scholarship and translation movements continue in the Mediterranean.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia (limited written sources beyond Mongol records)

events.title: Death of Francis of Assisi
events.description: Francis of Assisi, founder of the Franciscan order, dies on October 3, 1226. His death marked the end of a transformative life that had profoundly influenced medieval Christianity. Francis's emphasis on poverty, humility, and direct spiritual experience challenged the institutional Church and inspired a new form of religious devotion. His canonization followed quickly (1228), and the Franciscan order became one of the most influential religious movements of the medieval period, rivaling the Dominicans in importance.
key_figures: Francis of Assisi (founder of Franciscan order)

sources.name: Vita Prima by Thomas of Celano (written 1228–1229)

events.certainty_note: Francis's death date (October 3, 1226) is well-documented in ecclesiastical records and early biographical sources. The date is secure.
cross_references: Part of broader medieval religious reform (12th–13th centuries)

events.title: Mongol conquest of the Jin dynasty intensifies
events.description: Genghis Khan and the Mongol confederation continue their systematic conquest of the Jin dynasty. In 1226, major campaigns were launched against Jin territories, with Mongol forces capturing key fortifications and expanding their control. The Jin dynasty, weakened by internal strife and economic exhaustion, struggled to mount effective resistance. The Mongol conquest would culminate in the Jin dynasty's collapse by 1234, but 1226 represented a critical phase of the systematic military campaign.
events.certainty_note: The broad trajectory of Mongol conquest is secure; specific campaigns in 1226 are reconstructed from later chronicles with ±1–2 year uncertainty.

events.title: Reconquista: Ferdinand III of Castile captures Úbeda
events.description: Ferdinand III of Castile captures the city of Úbeda from Islamic control, a major victory in the Reconquista. Úbeda was a significant fortress and commercial center in the upper Guadalquivir valley. The capture represented the continued Christian advance into al-Andalus and weakened Islamic control of southern Iberia. Ferdinand III was one of the most successful Reconquista monarchs, and this victory was part of his broader campaign to expand Castilian territory.

sources.name: Chronica Latina de los Reyes de Castilla
Later Castilian chronicles and historiographic consensus

events.certainty_note: The capture of Úbeda is attested in Castilian chronicles, but the exact date is sometimes given as 1225 or 1226. The uncertainty is ±1 year.
cross_references: Led to 1227-004 (Ferdinand III's capture of Baeza)

events.title: Almohad decline: fragmentation accelerates in North Africa
events.description: The Almohad dynasty continues to fragment as regional governors and rival dynasties assert independence. By 1226, the Hafsid dynasty in Tunisia and the Marinid dynasty in Morocco were consolidating power, effectively ending Almohad control in these regions. The Almohad center of power in Marrakech weakened, and the dynasty's military and political authority eroded. This fragmentation accelerated the Christian Reconquista in Iberia and enabled the rise of successor Islamic dynasties.
events.certainty_note: The broad trajectory of Almohad decline is secure; specific events in 1226 are reconstructed from later sources with ±2–5 year uncertainty.
cross_references: Part of broader Almohad dynasty decline (1130–1269)

events.title: Khmer Empire: Jayavarman VII's reign continues; Angkor at peak influence
events.description: Jayavarman VII, the great builder-king of the Khmer Empire, continues his reign (r. 1181–1218 or later; dating uncertain). By 1226, Angkor represents the apex of Khmer architectural and administrative achievement, with massive temple complexes, hydraulic systems, and a sophisticated bureaucracy. The empire controls much of mainland Southeast Asia. However, some sources suggest Jayavarman VII's reign may have ended by this date, and succession disputes may have begun, though documentation is sparse.
key_figures: Jayavarman VII (Khmer King, if still reigning)
events.certainty_note: Khmer chronology for this period is uncertain; Jayavarman VII's reign dates are debated (some sources place his death as early as 1218, others as late as 1230s). Inscriptional evidence is fragmentary.
cross_references: Part of Khmer Empire's classical period (9th–13th centuries)

disconfirming_evidence: Francis of Assisi (1226-001) is often portrayed as a simple, humble saint, but modern scholarship emphasizes the complexity of his life and the tensions within the Franciscan movement. His emphasis on poverty was radical and sometimes controversial, and the order he founded quickly became institutionalized in ways that departed from his original vision. Additionally, some sources suggest that Francis's death date may have been October 4 rather than October 3, though October 3 is the traditional date. Regarding the Mongol conquest (1226-002), the exact campaigns and their dates are uncertain; later chronicles may have conflated or rearranged events. The Reconquista victories (1226-003) are sometimes portrayed as inevitable Christian triumphs, but they were achieved through sustained military effort and were not always permanent.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1226 is moderate but geographically uneven. Western European events (Francis's death, Reconquista) are well-attested in contemporary chronicles and ecclesiastical records. East Asian events (Mongol conquest, Jin dynasty) are known through later Chinese and Islamic compilations with ±1–2 year uncertainty. Islamic sources for North Africa and al-Andalus exist but are often preserved in later compilations. Southeast Asian, sub-Saharan African, and Eastern European history have minimal written documentation for this year. The surviving sources privilege elite political, military, and religious events; economic, social, and demographic changes are largely invisible.

graph_edges.note: Francis's death led to rapid canonization and expansion of Franciscan influence
Mongol campaigns of 1226 continued into 1227 with siege of Kaifeng
Ferdinand III's Úbeda victory enabled further Reconquista expansion
Almohad fragmentation continued under al-Ma'mun's weak reign
